speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 16, 1996. The survivor, Jack Gun, last name at birth also Gun. The interviewer, Chanan Posner. This interview is taking place in West Bloomfield, Michigan, United States of America. The language will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: The interviewer's name is Chanan Posner. Today's date is August the 17th, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Jack Gun in West Bloomfield, Michigan, in the United States. We're conducting the interview in the English language. A correction to the date. Today's date is August the 16th, 1996. Could you tell us your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Jack Gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-C-K G-U-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names or nicknames growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Europe, I was known as Yankel Gun. Same name, was pronounced different. In Europe, it's pronounced 'goon.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the city and country where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Rozyszcze, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's R-O-Z-Y-C-Z-Y-C-Z-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's eastern part of Poland. It's about 220 miles west of Kiev, Ukraine. The two bigger cities near my town were Lutsk, L-U-C-K, and Rovna, which was a very big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is it a big town? What kind of a town is Rozyszcze?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rozyszcze was a quite small town, with a population of approximately 15,000. And out of the 15,000, 11,000 were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers or sisters growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a brother 10 years older than me and a sister 7 years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name is Anszel. In America, he's called-- excuse me-- he's called Ansel. And my sister's name was Raizel, which is Rose.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your parents, what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Shmuel, and my mother's name was Sonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people approximately were there in your family, which includes aunts, uncles, first cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say approximately between 16 and 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many survived after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only ones survived were my brother and myself and a first cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories of what it was like growing up in Rozyszcze?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have very little memories. I was very young. Being born in 1934, I-- when the war broke, I was only five. And the only thing I remember is, you know, just running around, playing with neighbors and sometimes cousins that came over, and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these-- some of the neighbors, were these Jewish or non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had both. Most of them were Jewish, but we also had a gentile living not too far from us. I remember I used to play with the little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of occupation did your parents have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a merchant. He was involved in about three businesses. He had a store, retail store of fabrics, which was very-- a very popular business at the time, you know, sold all kind-- because people used to sew, make their own clothing, so he used to sell for suits and dresses and whatever. And he also was involved in a wholesale tobacco business with-- he was a partner with the mayor of our town. And one reason he was a partner with the mayor was because the Jews were not allowed to be involved in tobacco business, and the license was in the mayor's name. He was also involved in exporting eggs. So we were quite well-to-do-- my father was quite well-to-do for those times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the home that you lived in like? Could you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a very nice home for-- for those times, a single family home, quite large, with I believe three bedrooms and a nice living room and quite a nice kitchen. But we still didn't-- even though we were well-to-do, we still didn't have any indoor plumbing. That was not existing at the time in this part of our-- this part of the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other things that you remember that you were able to have access to that maybe other kids didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I remember as a little boy I had-- had no problem as far as food. And I had bananas and oranges and grapes, which used to be imported, and only the-- the people that had little money could afford. There are many people in our town that I'm sure their kids never saw it, because they couldn't afford it. They were quite poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship like with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean with the immediate family?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Yeah. Growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- well, my mother and father both worked. My mother was very active in the fabric store. And we had a-- a Jewish woman that lived in our house, came from a-- I believe she even came from a different town. And she was very-- came from poor parents and she looked for work. And she lived with us. And she used to take care of the household and used to take care of the children, used to cook. And I also remember my mother, when she came home, she used to play with me. And she used to try to feed me. I wasn't that great of an eater. She used to run around sometimes trying to force a spoonful of soup or a spoonful of sour cream, you know, to fatten me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a skinny kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was a skinny boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your brother and sister? Do you remember doing things with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was quite a bit of age difference. I was the youngest. And you know, my brother being 10 years older, he naturally played with kids his age. And my sister also, she was seven years older, so there was quite a bit of age difference. But they used to play with me. And, you know, I was the baby in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any memories of grandparents or other relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have memories of my-- both of my grandmothers. I never knew my-- my grandfathers on both sides were-- died before I was born, I believe. I remember my grandmothers were quite, you know-- I remember most of the stuff actually like a dream, you know. It was so-- being so young. But I do remember my grandmother from my mother's side used to live in a little town not too far from us called [POLISH] and she used to come to us quite often. She was a very, very warm, caring person, and always used to worry about her grandchildren and just-- even though she had a-- a son which was an invalid, and she used to have somebody stay with him for a day and she would come over to us and try to spend a little bit of time with her grandchildren. That's all she had. And my father's grandmother used to live in the same town. And she used to come to us sometimes for Friday night dinners. She was very religious, even though my father was-- wasn't a very religious man. I mean, he used to go to shul many Saturdays. And I think he did it mainly to please his mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what a Friday night was like? Any memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just vaguely, you know. I can remember them coming. And, you know-- and my mother used to light candles and my father used to make kiddish. And we had a regular Shabbos meal. And that's about all I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any kind of rituals that you had in the family, songs you used to sing or things you used to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I can recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what your friends were like that you used to play with when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very vaguely. I remember, you know, being out in the street and playing and-- or they'd come into the house. But real, real vivid memories I don't have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any particular memories that stand out for you, any incidents that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean from before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing in particular. Nothing in particular that I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how would you describe yourself as a kid? What kind of a personality did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really-- just an average an average Jewish kid growing up in a little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any political discussion between your parents or anyone else in the family about things-- about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure there was. You know, we had a radio and my father used to listen always to try to get the news and-- but I was too young to understand or to listen to it. I mean, it just-- it didn't concern me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was the relationship like between the Jews and the non-Jews in Rozyszcze?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, the relationship was not too bad. You know, the Ukrainians were never too sympathetic to the Jews. But-- and I would say in most of the cases, we didn't feel too much of it, not until the war broke out. During while it was still Poland, things were quite-- quite well for us. We didn't feel too much of it, especially being we were the majority, actually, in our town, because of being so many Jewish people. So the-- the Ukrainians or the Poles whoever were there, they sort of kept their distance and didn't bother us too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there ever any incidents of anti-Semitism that you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally did not. But I'm sure there were incidents. But like I said, I don't believe it was too bad up until the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do you know if anyone in your family had ever experienced any kind of anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, not that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened around the time that-- 1939, like right before that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1939, I believe in September of 1939, when the war broke out, the Germans came into our town just for a very short time, maybe for a couple months. And then Hitler and Stalin made the pact where they divided Poland in half. And our part became Russia, which was much better for the Jews, naturally. And when Russia came into our town, mainly what happened was that they nationalized everything. And since my father was a businessman and they-- they found out that, you know, he was quite wealthy for-- for those days, they took away all the keys from the businesses and they took over the businesses, naturally. But they didn't do anything bad to the Jews. And what they did is they-- they asked the working people and the people that used to work like for my father, asked them what kind of person my father was, and the other businesspeople naturally in town. And whoever they felt that was-- you know, was mistreating the-- the working people, wasn't a very good person, used to take advantage, they took them and shipped them to Russia, to Siberia. And it was our misfortune that my father was quite well-liked. He always did an awful lot for the working people. He always was very involved with the working community. In fact, my father established a-- a Jewish volksschule, for Jewish kids to go to learn about Judaism and about the Jewish language. And in fact, I understand he gave up part of our house for that. So he was very much liked by the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what that is, the volksschule?

speaker: interviewee
text: The volksschule was like-- strictly to-- to learn about Jewish-- the Jewish history and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's all that I know of. My brother, I believe, attended. I was too young. And as I was saying, he was very, very-- he was very known in town and had a very good name in town and people liked him. So because of that, they didn't ship us out to Siberia. And they gave my father a job in a hospital. And we lived quite nicely. I mean, not as nice as we did before. But nobody bothered us and things were going quite-- you know, we got accustomed to that type of life. You know, we're doing-- getting along with less things. I'm sure there was no more oranges and bananas and grapes in the house, but nobody was hungry and we had plenty of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was for how long? From when until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was from I would say end of '39, let's say maybe-- September the war broke out, so the-- the Germany came in for a couple of months. But they were quite civilized at the time, didn't do anything to us. And that probably was November or December of 1939. The Russians came in. And they were there up until June of 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you remember when-- seeing the Germans when they were marching in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I can remember looking out the window as a little boy, you know. And-- and there was a-- you know, everybody was-- seemed to have been very frightened. There was a scare-- a scary feeling in the house, and-- and I'm sure in all the houses, because I'm sure our parents have heard what the Germans were up to and what they were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- even as being as young as I was, not quite seven, I remember being scared. I don't know from what or I didn't realize from what. But that-- that scary feeling was right in there. What did they look like? What did the soldiers look like to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they looked like angry people. They looked like, you know, they were coming in to-- to-- to do something bad to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the-- the uniforms that they had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I can visualize the uniforms. They came in. They were dark green. And they were riding on motorcycles and then tanks and, you know, just when-- you know, like when an army comes in to invade another country. And they all-- all had, you know, mean, mean looks-- looks on their face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever try to explain to you what was going on or was-- did you ever ask any questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't think I did. But you know, in-- in my own little way I guess I understood to a certain extent as much as a seven-year-old can understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when the Russians came?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians came, yes, was a different feeling. First [INAUDIBLE] well, I was also younger and so-- you know, I was two years younger. I don't-- don't recall of having any scare, of being frightened, maybe because I was two years younger and I didn't understand, or maybe the talk in the house was different. But there was quite a difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the-- the uniforms that they wore?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I don't really recall the colors of the uniforms. But they were a friendlier-- a friendlier group of soldiers, and they weren't as-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how to explain. They just weren't that scary-looking. Or maybe we got that from our families-- from what they were talking, maybe we got that-- you know, that notion in ourselves that, you know, we knew to be-- that they're out there to get us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any-- ever any attempt or discussion about possibly leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I'll tell you, I know that a lot of people when the Russians left, many of our-- quite a few people from our town went right along with the Russian army. In fact, our first cousin that survived, he ran away with the Russian army and he survived in Russia. But I think my-- my father was too much of a family man. And he had an older mother. And my mother had her older mother. And I think they just didn't think of leaving, which was a grave, grave mistake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an opportunity to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure if you wanted to. I'm sure it was hard to-- [INAUDIBLE] to grab a family with three kids. Mainly people that left were single people, people that were not married, people that, you know, were still-- had no responsibilities. Those are the ones that took off.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the first cousin that you said left to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shika Dichter. Yehoshua was his Hebrew name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other family members or friends that-- that had either left or tried to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken off? Yes, there were quite a few people from my town, you know, that I didn't know personally. My brother knows them. And they took off quite a bit. And the majority of them survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when they left, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right when the-- left right when-- just before the Germans came into our town, left together with the Russian army in 1941, probably in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: So until '41, it was-- it was OK to leave. It was not a problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Til '41 I'm sure you could have left to Russia. But, you know, the Jewish people in Poland also heard that things weren't that great in Russia, you know, as far as you know, that there were shortage of food and things weren't that great. So-- but they didn't realize what is coming soon after. If they would have realized that, I think many more would have left. They just couldn't visualize the brutality of the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know that my father used to listen-- as I was saying, he used to listen to the news. And he couldn't believe that the German army was gonna occupy every little-- Rozyszcze? I mean, who wants to come into Rozyszcze, I mean, a little-- a little hick town, you know? But-- but unfortunately, they covered every little city. Every little village that had any kind of Jewish population, they were there. So during this time that the Russians were occupying your town, were you going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was too young. They started-- I believe in our part of the country they started school at the age of seven, so I didn't have any kind of schooling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have memories of-- of when the Russians were-- were there, what it-- what it was like, the atmosphere?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, it wasn't much different as before. I mean, the Russians did not bother us. They didn't harass us. And-- I-- I was free. And I mean, I wasn't afraid to go outside. I did the same things as I did before they came in, like only probably difference was that I didn't have as good of food as I had before, which, you know, being young, you don't-- you don't pay much attention to that. But there was no difference in my upbringing or in my growing up. I mean, I didn't feel anything different from when the Russians took over from-- from before as being Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after when the Germans came in? could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, when the Germans came in in June, they sort of-- you know, the Germans had very good technique to demoralize the Jewish population. And this was I think the-- the biggest fact. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were-- they put us down so much. And the-- the demoralization of the Jewish people, the-- the scare that they put into us, that affected us an awful lot. And approximately two or three months after they were in, they came out with notices. And they came with loudspeakers and told all the Jews to come out of their houses and take everything they can carry, their possessions, only thing that they could carry with them. And we all had to march out and form, you know, lines. And they marched us towards one part of town, which they separated as the ghetto. And they put all the Jews from Rozyszcze into a ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I also can remember walking with my parents and my brother and sisters, you know, with our heads down, you know, afraid to even look up, just like a herd of sheep or a herd of cattle marching to the pasture, you know. They marched us into that town, into that part of the city. Usually, you know, it was the dilapidated part of town. And they put us into-- into a house, like three, four families in one little house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you react when-- when you heard this, when they're saying over the loudspeakers about how you can just take whatever you have and-- and leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just fright, fear, didn't know what to expect. My brother tells me that I asked my parents. You know, I said, hey, why is the-- I imagined here I used to play with a little boy that wasn't Jewish. And I said to him-- I asked my parents, look, why isn't-- why isn't this boy-- I don't remember his name-- why isn't he coming out? Why isn't he going with us? And all answer-- the only answer they could give me is because he's not Jewish. This is what they're doing to Jewish people right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you take that answer? I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know how I-- I took it with a-- I didn't understand. I couldn't understand what it means. It was strange to me. I didn't know the difference about being Jewish or not being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like when you were walking towards the ghetto? Was this everyone, all the Jews from the town walking all together at once?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jews from our city, walking all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if there were other people who were watching while you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I'm sure there-- there were the-- the Ukrainians and the Poles were very happy. They were [NON-ENGLISH] what they're doing to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember people standing, you know, on sidewalks and watching. And some of them were yelling or talking about it. But I don't know what they were saying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far was the Rozyszcze ghetto from where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too far. I would say maybe two-- two-- two kilometers, three kilometers. The city wasn't that big to begin with, so it wasn't that far of a walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the Rozyszcze ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of Tape number 1.
